stem: A 55-year-old man with seropositive rheumatoid arthritis on long-term methotrexate presents with acute onset of bilateral hand weakness and difficulty walking. Examination reveals upper motor neuron signs in all four extremities with hyperreflexia and upgoing plantar responses. Flexion-extension cervical spine radiographs show an atlantodental interval of 10 mm. What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cervical radiculopathy from degenerative disc disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rheumatoid vasculitis of the spinal cord

explanation:
ATLANTOAXIAL SUBLUXATION — RA EMERGENCY

Correct Answer: A — Atlantoaxial subluxation with cervical myelopathy

Atlantoaxial subluxation is a life-threatening complication of rheumatoid arthritis, caused by pannus erosion of the transverse ligament of C1 that normally stabilizes the odontoid process (dens) of C2. The normal atlantodental interval (ADI) is <3 mm in adults; ADI >9 mm indicates complete transverse ligament disruption with high risk of spinal cord compression. The presenting symptoms of cervical myelopathy (bilateral upper motor neuron signs, spastic quadriparesis, hyperreflexia, Babinski signs) indicate cord compression and constitute a surgical emergency. Treatment requires urgent cervical stabilization and posterior C1-C2 fusion. All RA patients should have cervical spine screening before intubation for any surgical procedure.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Methotrexate causes myelosuppression and hepatotoxicity but not cervical myelopathy
• C: Radiculopathy causes lower motor neuron findings (weakness, decreased reflexes) in a dermatomal pattern — not bilateral UMN signs
• D: Rheumatoid vasculitis typically affects peripheral nerves (mononeuritis multiplex), skin, and viscera — not the spinal cord directly

Ref: StatPearls NBK535405 | Verified 2025

stem: A 58-year-old man with obesity and hypertension presents with sudden-onset excruciating pain, redness, warmth, and swelling of the right first MTP joint that woke him from sleep. He cannot tolerate even a bedsheet touching the joint. He consumed a large steak dinner with several beers last night. He takes hydrochlorothiazide for blood pressure.Vital SignsTemp 38.0°CHR 92LabsSerum urate 9.8 mg/dLWBC 12,500CRP 48 mg/LSynovial fluid Needle-shaped, NEGATIVELY birefringent crystals under polarized microscopy; WBC 42,000 What confirms the diagnosis, and what distinguishes this from pseudogout?

choices.text: Septic arthritis — fever with joint WBC >50,000 requires IV antibiotics
Pseudogout (CPPD) — crystal arthropathy in an elderly patient
Acute gout — NEEDLE-shaped, NEGATIVELY birefringent (yellow when parallel to compensator) monosodium urate crystals on
Cellulitis overlying the MTP joint — erythema and warmth indicate skin infection

explanation:
Correct Answer: Gout vs pseudogout: both are crystal arthropathies but with OPPOSITE crystal properties. Gout: monosodium urate (MSU) crystals = NEEDLE-shaped, NEGATIVELY birefringent (yellow parallel to compensator). Pseudogout (CPPD): calcium pyrophosphate crystals = RHOMBOID-shaped, POSITIVELY birefringent (blue parallel to compensator). Joint aspiration with polarized microscopy is the GOLD STANDARD for diagnosis. Classic gout presentation: podagra (1st MTP), sudden onset, triggered by purine-rich foods, alcohol, dehydration, or medications that raise urate (thiazides, loop diuretics).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Sudden-onset 1st MTP pain (podagra) = most classic gout presentation
- Triggers present: red meat (purines), beer (purines + impaired urate excretion), thiazide (reduces renal urate clearance)
- Needle-shaped + negatively birefringent = MSU crystals = gout (not CPPD)
- Synovial WBC 42,000 = inflammatory but below typical septic range (>50,000)
- Serum urate 9.8 (elevated) supports diagnosis BUT can be normal during acute flare (don't rely on serum urate alone)

Evidence: Gout - StatPearls - Ragab G, et al. StatPearls. NBK546606. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Septic arthritis: Septic arthritis is the critical differential. Synovial WBC >50,000 is suggestive (not diagnostic) of septic arthritis, but gout can also produce WBC >50,000. The KEY: joint fluid Gram stain and culture rule out infection; polarized microscopy showing MSU crystals confirms gout. Note: gout and septic arthritis CAN coexist — always send culture.
- Pseudogout (CPPD): CPPD crystals are RHOMBOID-shaped and POSITIVELY birefringent (blue parallel to compensator) — opposite of gout. CPPD typically affects the KNEE and WRIST (not 1st MTP), occurs in elderly patients, and is associated with hemochromatosis, hyperparathyroidism, and hypomagnesemia.
- Reactive arthritis: Reactive arthritis (formerly Reiter syndrome) is a post-infectious asymmetric oligoarthritis (usually large joints) that follows urethritis or dysentery. It does NOT show crystals on joint aspiration and is associated with HLA-B27. The crystal findings rule this out.
- Cellulitis overlying the MTP joint: Cellulitis can mimic gout with erythema and warmth, but cellulitis causes diffuse spreading erythema without the intense joint effusion and synovial crystals. Joint aspiration differentiates: gout shows crystals in synovial fluid; cellulitis does not produce a joint effusion.

Reference: Gout - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK546606/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: An 82-year-old woman presents with acute pain, swelling, and warmth of the right KNEE that developed over 24 hours. She has a history of osteoarthritis and recent hospitalization for pneumonia. No dietary triggers.LabsSynovial fluid RHOMBOID-shaped, POSITIVELY birefringent crystals (blue parallel to compensator); WBC 28,000Knee XR Chondrocalcinosis (calcification of meniscal cartilage)Serum urate 5.2 mg/dL (normal) The parent question showed needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals in the 1st MTP = gout. How do these different crystals change the diagnosis?

choices.text: Gout — all crystal arthropathies are gout
Pseudogout (CPPD disease) — RHOMBOID-shaped, POSITIVELY birefringent calcium pyrophosphate crystals;
Septic arthritis — acute monoarthritis with fever in an elderly hospitalized patient
Osteoarthritis flare — exacerbation of pre-existing OA
Rheumatoid arthritis of the knee — autoimmune joint inflammation

explanation:
Correct Answer: THE PIVOT: Parent showed NEEDLE + NEGATIVELY birefringent + 1st MTP = gout. Here: RHOMBOID + POSITIVELY birefringent + KNEE = pseudogout (CPPD). Memory aid: Pseudogout is Positively birefringent (P-P). Additional distinguishers: CPPD affects large joints (knee > wrist), occurs in elderly women (gout: middle-aged men), shows chondrocalcinosis on X-ray, and is triggered by acute medical illness, surgery, or hospitalization (not dietary purines). Associated metabolic conditions: hemochromatosis (“iron overload”), hyperparathyroidism, hypomagnesemia, hypothyroidism (the 4 H’s).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Rhomboid + positively birefringent = CPPD crystals (opposite of MSU)
- Knee involvement (not 1st MTP) = classic CPPD distribution
- Chondrocalcinosis on X-ray = pathognomonic for CPPD crystal deposition
- Serum urate normal (5.2) = not hyperuricemia-driven
- Elderly woman + recent hospitalization = classic CPPD trigger (acute illness in elderly)

Evidence: CPPD - StatPearls - Rosenthal AK, et al. StatPearls. NBK540157. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Gout: Gout crystals are NEEDLE-shaped and NEGATIVELY birefringent — opposite of this patient's rhomboid, positively birefringent crystals. Gout classically affects the 1st MTP (not knee), occurs in middle-aged men, and is associated with hyperuricemia (urate here is normal at 5.2).
- Septic arthritis: Septic arthritis is the critical differential in any acute monoarthritis, especially in a hospitalized elderly patient. However, the crystal identification on polarized microscopy makes CPPD the primary diagnosis. ALWAYS send Gram stain and culture alongside crystal analysis — septic arthritis and CPPD can coexist.
- Osteoarthritis flare: OA can cause knee pain and effusion, but OA fluid is NON-inflammatory (WBC <2,000) and does NOT show crystals. This patient has inflammatory fluid (WBC 28,000) with CPPD crystals. However, OA and CPPD frequently coexist, as cartilage degeneration in OA promotes CPP crystal deposition.
- Rheumatoid arthritis: RA typically causes symmetric polyarthritis of small joints (MCPs, PIPs, wrists), not acute monoarthritis of the knee. RA synovial fluid does not contain crystals. The crystal findings and X-ray chondrocalcinosis are diagnostic of CPPD.

Reference: CPPD - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK540157/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 28-year-old African American woman presents with 3 months of fatigue, joint pain (symmetric small joint polyarthritis of hands and wrists), a photosensitive malar rash (sparing the nasolabial folds), oral ulcers, and pleuritic chest pain. She also reports new-onset foamy urine.LabsANA Positive (1:640, homogeneous pattern)Anti-dsDNA Positive (highly elevated)Anti-Smith PositiveC3 42 mg/dL (low)C4 8 mg/dL (low)UA 3+ protein, RBC castsCr 1.4 mg/dLCBC WBC 3,200 (leukopenia), Hgb 10.2, Plt 118,000ESR 68 mm/h What is the diagnosis, and which findings indicate organ-threatening disease requiring urgent management?

choices.text: Rheumatoid arthritis — symmetric polyarthritis of small joints
SLE with lupus nephritis — the malar rash (sparing nasolabial folds), anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith antibodies (most specific for SLE), low
Drug-induced lupus — anti-histone antibody positive lupus from medication exposure
Mixed connective tissue disease — overlap syndrome with anti-U1 RNP antibodies
Dermatomyositis — photosensitive rash with muscle weakness

explanation:
Correct Answer: SLE diagnosis: multisystem autoimmune disease. Key antibodies: ANA (sensitive, not specific — 98% of SLE is ANA-positive), anti-dsDNA (specific, correlates with disease activity and nephritis), anti-Smith (most specific for SLE). Low complements (C3, C4) indicate active disease (immune complex consumption). The ALARM findings here: proteinuria + RBC casts = lupus nephritis (Class III/IV GN), which is the leading cause of morbidity/mortality in SLE and requires urgent renal biopsy + induction immunosuppression (mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide + steroids).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Young African American woman = highest SLE risk demographic
- Malar rash SPARING nasolabial folds = classic butterfly rash (distinguishes from rosacea which involves nasolabial folds)
- Anti-dsDNA + anti-Smith = most specific SLE antibodies
- Low C3/C4 = active complement consumption from immune complexes
- Proteinuria + RBC casts = glomerulonephritis (lupus nephritis) = URGENT
- Leukopenia + thrombocytopenia = SLE hematologic involvement

Evidence: SLE - StatPearls - Justiz Vaillant AA, et al. StatPearls. NBK535405. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Rheumatoid arthritis: RA causes symmetric polyarthritis of small joints (overlapping with SLE), but RA is EROSIVE (joint destruction), does NOT cause malar rash, oral ulcers, or nephritis, and is associated with anti-CCP and RF (not anti-dsDNA/Smith). SLE arthritis is typically non-erosive.
- Drug-induced lupus: Drug-induced lupus (procainamide, hydralazine, isoniazid, minocycline) has anti-HISTONE antibodies (not anti-dsDNA/Smith), does NOT typically cause nephritis or severe cytopenias, and resolves after stopping the offending drug. This patient has no drug exposure and has nephritis.
- Mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD): MCTD is defined by high-titer anti-U1 RNP antibodies with overlapping features of SLE, scleroderma, and polymyositis. This patient has anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith (SLE-specific) without features of scleroderma or myositis. MCTD typically has puffy hands and Raynaud phenomenon.
- Dermatomyositis: Dermatomyositis causes photosensitive rashes (heliotrope rash, Gottron papules) but with PROXIMAL MUSCLE WEAKNESS and elevated CK. SLE does NOT cause proximal weakness or elevated CK. The malar rash pattern (butterfly, sparing nasolabial folds) and anti-dsDNA are specific to SLE.

Reference: SLE - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK535405/ [Accessed 2025]

Correct Answer: Rheumatoid arthritis: symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis primarily affecting MCPs, PIPs, and wrists with DIP sparing (DIP involvement suggests OA or PsA). Morning stiffness >60 minutes distinguishes inflammatory from degenerative arthritis (OA: Anti-CCP is the most SPECIFIC antibody for RA (95-98% specificity vs RF 85% specificity). RF is sensitive but non-specific (positive in infections, other autoimmune diseases, elderly). Dual positivity (RF + anti-CCP) = very high specificity and predicts erosive disease.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Symmetric MCP/PIP involvement with DIP sparing = classic RA distribution
- Morning stiffness 90 min (>60 min = inflammatory arthritis)
- Anti-CCP strongly positive = most specific RA antibody (95-98%)
- RF positive = supports diagnosis but less specific
- Boggy synovitis = active synovial inflammation (pannus formation)
- Early XR changes: periarticular osteopenia precedes erosions

Evidence: Rheumatoid Arthritis - StatPearls - Chauhan K, et al. StatPearls. NBK441999. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Osteoarthritis: OA affects DIP joints (Heberden nodes), PIP joints (Bouchard nodes), and the 1st CMC (base of thumb) — NOT MCPs. OA has brief morning stiffness (<30 min), bony (not boggy) enlargement, and normal inflammatory markers. OA is non-inflammatory (no synovitis, no elevated ESR/CRP).
- SLE arthritis: SLE can cause symmetric polyarthritis (overlapping with RA), but SLE arthritis is typically non-erosive (Jaccoud arthropathy = reducible deformities without erosions). SLE has multi-system involvement (rash, nephritis, serositis) and different antibodies (anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith). RF can be positive in SLE but anti-CCP is specific to RA.
- Psoriatic arthritis: PsA can cause hand arthritis but typically involves DIP joints (RA spares DIPs), presents with dactylitis (sausage digits), enthesitis, and asymmetric distribution. PsA is associated with psoriasis (skin/nail changes). RF and anti-CCP are typically NEGATIVE in PsA.
- Viral polyarthritis: Parvovirus B19 can cause symmetric polyarthritis mimicking RA in adults (especially women), but it is ACUTE (lasting 2-3 weeks, not 4 months), self-limited, and RF/anti-CCP negative. Parvovirus IgM confirms recent infection. The 4-month duration and strongly positive anti-CCP rule this out.

Reference: RA - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441999/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 52-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and a recent skin abscess presents with a hot, swollen, extremely painful right knee. He cannot bear weight or flex the joint. Passive range of motion elicits severe pain.Vital SignsBP 108/64HR 112Temp 39.4°CSynovial fluidAppearance Purulent (opaque yellow-green)WBC 92,000/mm³ (>90% PMNs)Gram stain Gram-positive cocci in clustersCrystals NoneCulture Pending What is the most critical management?

choices.text: NSAIDs and colchicine for crystal arthropathy until culture results return
Septic arthritis — emergent joint drainage (arthrocentesis or surgical washout) plus empiric IV vancomycin (MRSA
Observation and repeat aspiration in 48 hours
Oral antibiotics are sufficient for joint infections
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection to reduce inflammation

explanation:
Correct Answer: Septic arthritis is a surgical emergency. A hot, swollen monoarthritis with purulent synovial fluid (WBC >50,000, >75% PMNs) + positive Gram stain = septic arthritis until proven otherwise. Management: (1) Emergent joint drainage (repeated arthrocentesis or arthroscopic/open surgical washout), (2) Empiric IV antibiotics immediately: vancomycin (MRSA) + ceftriaxone (gram-negatives). Adjust based on culture. Staphylococcus aureus is the #1 cause in adults. Without prompt treatment: irreversible cartilage destruction within 24-48 hours.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Hot, swollen, extremely painful monoarthritis with fever = septic until proven otherwise
- Synovial WBC 92,000 (>50,000 highly suggestive) with >90% PMNs
- Gram-positive cocci in clusters = S. aureus (most common cause)
- No crystals = not gout/pseudogout (but always check — crystals and infection can coexist)
- Diabetes + recent skin abscess = risk factors for hematogenous seeding
- Delay → irreversible cartilage destruction; joint damage begins within 24-48 hours

Evidence: Septic Arthritis - StatPearls - Long B, et al. StatPearls. NBK459259. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- NSAIDs and colchicine for crystal arthropathy: No crystals were found on polarized microscopy, ruling out gout/pseudogout as the primary diagnosis. More importantly, treating a septic joint with anti-inflammatories instead of antibiotics and drainage risks irreversible joint destruction and potentially fatal sepsis.
- Observation and repeat aspiration in 48 hours: Waiting 48 hours for a septic joint would allow irreversible cartilage destruction. Septic arthritis is a time-sensitive emergency requiring SAME-DAY drainage and antibiotics. Every hour of delay worsens outcomes.
- Oral antibiotics: Oral antibiotics provide insufficient joint penetration for the initial treatment of septic arthritis. IV antibiotics are required for adequate bactericidal levels in the joint space. Oral step-down may be considered after clinical improvement (typically after 2 weeks of IV therapy).
- Intra-articular corticosteroid injection: Injecting corticosteroids into an infected joint is CONTRAINDICATED — it suppresses the local immune response and worsens the infection. Corticosteroid injection is appropriate for non-infectious conditions (OA, RA flare, crystal arthropathy) but ONLY after infection has been ruled out.

Reference: Septic Arthritis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459259/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with 2 weeks of new-onset severe unilateral headache, jaw claudication (pain with chewing that resolves with rest), scalp tenderness, and an episode of transient monocular vision loss (amaurosis fugax) in the right eye lasting 5 minutes yesterday. Exam shows a tender, nodular, non-pulsatile right temporal artery.LabsESR 98 mm/h (markedly elevated)CRP 8.4 mg/dLPlatelets 410,000 (reactive thrombocytosis)Hgb 11.0 g/dL What is the time-critical next step?

choices.text: Temporal artery biopsy first — do not start treatment without histologic confirmation
Start high-dose corticosteroids IMMEDIATELY (prednisone 60-80 mg/day or IV methylprednisolone 1g/day x3 if visual symptoms) —
MRI brain to evaluate for stroke before starting treatment
Start methotrexate as the first-line steroid-sparing agent
Low-dose aspirin alone is sufficient for visual symptoms

explanation:
Correct Answer: Giant cell arteritis with visual symptoms is a medical emergency. Amaurosis fugax (transient monocular vision loss) is a WARNING that permanent blindness from anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION) is imminent. Start high-dose steroids IMMEDIATELY — do NOT wait for biopsy. Temporal artery biopsy remains diagnostic even after 2 weeks of steroid treatment (inflammation takes time to resolve). Visual symptoms = IV methylprednisolone 1g/day x3 days followed by oral prednisone. Without visual symptoms: oral prednisone 60-80 mg/day. Biopsy ASAP (within 2 weeks) but NEVER delays treatment.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Age >50 + new headache + jaw claudication + elevated ESR = classic GCA triad
- Amaurosis fugax = ophthalmic artery inflammation causing transient retinal ischemia
- Without treatment: 15-20% progress to permanent bilateral blindness
- Non-pulsatile temporal artery = vessel occlusion from inflammation
- ESR 98 (markedly elevated) + CRP 8.4 + reactive thrombocytosis = systemic inflammation
- Start steroids NOW; biopsy within 2 weeks (steroids don't immediately normalize histology)

Evidence: Giant Cell Arteritis - StatPearls - Dua A, et al. StatPearls. NBK459376. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Temporal artery biopsy first: NEVER delay treatment for biopsy when visual symptoms are present. Amaurosis fugax means the ophthalmic artery is actively inflamed — permanent blindness can occur at any moment. Steroids should be started WITHIN HOURS of clinical suspicion. Biopsy can be performed within 2 weeks while the patient is already on treatment.
- MRI brain to evaluate for stroke: Amaurosis fugax in GCA is from ophthalmic artery inflammation (retinal ischemia), not cerebral embolism. While GCA can cause vertebrobasilar stroke, the presentation here (temporal headache, jaw claudication, transient monocular vision loss) is classic GCA. Brain MRI delays life-saving steroid initiation.
- Methotrexate first-line: Methotrexate is a steroid-sparing agent used to facilitate steroid tapering, NOT as initial treatment. The acute emergency requires HIGH-DOSE CORTICOSTEROIDS immediately. Tocilizumab has largely replaced methotrexate as the preferred steroid-sparing agent for GCA.
- Low-dose aspirin alone: Low-dose aspirin is ADJUNCTIVE (added to steroids for vascular protection) but is completely insufficient as sole treatment for GCA with visual symptoms. Without high-dose steroids to suppress the arterial inflammation, the patient will likely lose permanent vision.

Reference: GCA - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459376/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 55-year-old man presents with 2 months of bloody rhinorrhea, recurrent sinusitis, hemoptysis with pulmonary nodules on CXR, and now progressive renal failure.LabsCr 3.8 mg/dL (was 1.0 three months ago)UA 3+ blood, 2+ protein, RBC castsc-ANCA (PR3) Strongly positivep-ANCA (MPO) NegativeCXR Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules, some with cavitationHgb 9.2 g/dL What is the diagnosis, and what is the organ-threatening complication requiring immediate treatment?

choices.text: Lung cancer with paraneoplastic renal disease
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA, formerly Wegener) — c-ANCA/PR3-positive small vessel vasculitis

explanation:
Correct Answer: GPA (granulomatosis with polyangiitis): classic triad of upper airway + lungs + kidneys. c-ANCA (anti-PR3) is the defining antibody (90% in active generalized GPA). The renal component = rapidly progressive (crescentic) glomerulonephritis (RPGN) — pauci-immune (few immune deposits). RBC casts + rapidly rising creatinine = RENAL EMERGENCY. Treatment: induction with rituximab or cyclophosphamide + high-dose steroids; maintenance with rituximab or azathioprine. Plasmapheresis for severe renal disease (Cr >5.7) or pulmonary hemorrhage.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Bloody rhinorrhea + sinusitis = upper airway involvement (GPA hallmark)
- Cavitary pulmonary nodules = granulomatous lung disease (classic GPA)
- Rising Cr (1.0 → 3.8) + RBC casts = rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis
- c-ANCA/PR3 strongly positive = GPA (not MPA which is p-ANCA/MPO)
- Complete triad: upper airway + lung + kidney = GPA
- This is pauci-immune RPGN (type III RPGN) — urgent immunosuppression needed

Evidence: GPA - StatPearls - Lutalo PM, et al. StatPearls. NBK557827. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Lung cancer with paraneoplastic renal disease: While cavitary lung lesions can represent malignancy, the triad of upper airway + lung + kidney involvement with strongly positive c-ANCA/PR3 is pathognomonic for GPA. Paraneoplastic membranous nephropathy causes nephrotic syndrome (heavy proteinuria), not RPGN with RBC casts.
- Goodpasture syndrome (anti-GBM disease): Goodpasture causes pulmonary hemorrhage + RPGN (pulmonary-renal syndrome) but does NOT involve the upper airway (no sinusitis or bloody rhinorrhea). It is associated with anti-GBM antibodies (not c-ANCA), and the lung involvement is diffuse alveolar hemorrhage (not cavitary nodules).
- Microscopic polyangiitis (MPA): MPA is p-ANCA/MPO-positive (not c-ANCA/PR3) and causes RPGN + pulmonary hemorrhage similar to GPA, but MPA does NOT involve the upper airway and does NOT form granulomas. This patient has upper airway disease and cavitary nodules (granulomas) = GPA, not MPA.
- IgA vasculitis (HSP): IgA vasculitis causes palpable purpura + arthritis + abdominal pain + nephritis, primarily in children. It is IgA-mediated (not ANCA-associated). The upper airway disease, cavitary lung nodules, and c-ANCA positivity are specific to GPA.

Reference: GPA - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK557827/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 48-year-old woman with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis (scleroderma) diagnosed 2 years ago presents with sudden-onset severe headache, blurred vision, and oliguria. She was recently started on prednisone 30 mg/day for worsening skin thickening.Vital SignsBP 220/130HR 110LabsCr 4.2 mg/dL (was 1.1 last month)Hgb 8.4 g/dLSmear SchistocytesPlatelets 78,000UA 2+ protein, few RBC castsLDH 580 U/L What is the diagnosis, and what is the life-saving treatment?

choices.text: Malignant hypertension from unrelated essential hypertension — start IV nitroprusside
Scleroderma renal crisis — ACE inhibitors (captopril) are LIFE-SAVING and should be started immediately despite the acute kidney injury;
TTP — schistocytes and thrombocytopenia require plasma exchange
Increase prednisone dose to control the scleroderma flare
ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in acute kidney injury — use calcium channel blockers instead

explanation:
Correct Answer: Scleroderma renal crisis (SRC): acute onset hypertensive emergency + AKI + MAHA in a patient with diffuse cutaneous scleroderma. ACE inhibitors (captopril) are the LIFE-SAVING treatment — this is the ONE scenario where ACE inhibitors are started DESPITE acute kidney injury. Captopril reverses the renin-angiotensin activation driving the renal vasospasm. Before ACE inhibitors, SRC was uniformly fatal; now 60-80% survive. Corticosteroids (≥15 mg/day prednisone) are a major TRIGGER for SRC. The schistocytes + thrombocytopenia are from renal thrombotic microangiopathy (similar mechanism to TTP/HUS but localized to the kidneys).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Diffuse scleroderma + sudden hypertensive emergency + AKI = scleroderma renal crisis
- Recent prednisone 30 mg/day = known SRC trigger (steroids ≥15 mg/day increase risk 2-3x)
- Schistocytes + thrombocytopenia = MAHA from renal microangiopathy
- BP 220/130 + Cr 1.1 → 4.2 = acute renal crisis
- ACE inhibitor (captopril) = LIFE-SAVING (blocks renin-angiotensin, reverses renal vasospasm)
- Do NOT withhold ACE inhibitor for elevated Cr — this is the one exception

Evidence: Scleroderma Renal Crisis - StatPearls - Woodworth TG, et al. StatPearls. NBK532282. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Malignant hypertension from essential hypertension: While the presentation resembles malignant hypertension, the context (diffuse scleroderma + recent corticosteroid use + MAHA) makes SRC the specific diagnosis. The treatment differs: SRC specifically requires ACE inhibitors (captopril); generic antihypertensives like nitroprusside don't address the underlying RAAS activation.
- TTP: TTP does cause MAHA + thrombocytopenia (overlapping with SRC), and both have schistocytes. However, TTP has NORMAL PT/fibrinogen and is treated with plasma exchange. SRC is distinguished by the scleroderma context, extreme hypertension, and AKI. SRC is treated with ACE inhibitors, not plasma exchange.
- Increase prednisone dose: DANGEROUS. Corticosteroids (≥15 mg/day) are a major TRIGGER for SRC. Increasing the dose would worsen the renal crisis. The prednisone should be DECREASED or stopped. This is one of the most important teaching points: avoid moderate-high dose steroids in diffuse scleroderma.
- ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in AKI: This is the ONE major exception to the general caution about ACE inhibitors in AKI. In SRC, ACE inhibitors are LIFE-SAVING because they directly interrupt the pathologic renin-angiotensin activation driving the crisis. Withholding ACE inhibitors is associated with significantly higher mortality and need for permanent dialysis.

Reference: Scleroderma Renal Crisis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532282/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 24-year-old man presents with 8 months of progressive low back pain and stiffness that is WORSE in the morning (>60 min of stiffness) and IMPROVES with exercise. Pain wakes him in the second half of the night. He has bilateral buttock pain alternating sides. Exam shows limited lumbar flexion (Schober test: 2 cm expansion instead of normal ≥5 cm) and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints.LabsHLA_B27 PositiveESR 42 mm/hCRP 2.4 mg/dLRF NegativeAnti-CCP NegativeSI joint MRI Bilateral sacroiliitis with bone marrow edema What is the diagnosis, and what distinguishes inflammatory back pain from mechanical?

choices.text: Mechanical low back pain — the most common cause of back pain in young adults
Ankylosing spondylitis — inflammatory back pain (onset <40, insidious, IMPROVES with exercise, morning stiffness >30 min, night
Rheumatoid arthritis of the spine — inflammatory markers are elevated
Lumbar disc herniation — radiating pain in a young man
Fibromyalgia — chronic widespread pain with morning stiffness

explanation:
Correct Answer: Inflammatory vs mechanical back pain: inflammatory back pain has 5 key features: (1) Age of onset IMPROVES with exercise (opposite of mechanical), (4) Morning stiffness >30 minutes, (5) Night pain (especially second half). ≥4 of 5 = inflammatory back pain. Ankylosing spondylitis (AS): axial spondyloarthropathy, HLA-B27 associated (90%), bilateral sacroiliitis is the hallmark. SERONEGATIVE (RF/anti-CCP negative — distinguishes from RA). Schober test <5 cm = reduced lumbar flexion from spinal inflammation/fusion.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Age 24 + insidious onset + improves with exercise + morning stiffness >60 min + night pain = all 5 inflammatory criteria met
- Bilateral sacroiliitis on MRI (bone marrow edema) = AS hallmark
- HLA-B27 positive (90% of AS patients, but 8% of general population are HLA-B27+)
- RF/anti-CCP negative = seronegative spondyloarthropathy (not RA)
- Schober test 2 cm (<5 cm normal) = restricted lumbar flexion
- Alternating buttock pain = sacroiliac joint inflammation

Evidence: Ankylosing Spondylitis - StatPearls - Wenker KJ, et al. StatPearls. NBK470173. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Mechanical low back pain: Mechanical back pain WORSENS with activity and IMPROVES with rest (opposite of inflammatory). Mechanical pain does not cause prolonged morning stiffness (>30 min), does not wake patients at night, and does not show sacroiliitis on imaging. The pattern here is classic inflammatory.
- Rheumatoid arthritis of the spine: RA affects the CERVICAL spine (atlantoaxial subluxation) but does NOT cause sacroiliitis or lumbar/thoracic disease. RA is seropositive (RF+, anti-CCP+). This patient is seronegative with bilateral sacroiliitis = spondyloarthropathy.
- Lumbar disc herniation: Disc herniation causes radicular pain (shooting down the leg in a dermatomal pattern, positive straight leg raise), not diffuse inflammatory back pain. It is mechanical (worsens with sitting/bending, improves with rest). No sacroiliitis on imaging.
- Fibromyalgia: Fibromyalgia causes widespread pain, fatigue, and stiffness, but it is a CENTRAL sensitization syndrome with no objective inflammatory findings (normal ESR, CRP, no sacroiliitis on imaging). This patient has elevated inflammatory markers and objective sacroiliitis on MRI.

Reference: AS - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470173/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with 6 weeks of bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain and stiffness that is worst in the morning (lasting >1 hour) and improves with activity. She has difficulty raising her arms above her head to brush her hair and getting out of a chair. She has no joint swelling or muscle weakness on formal strength testing. She also reports fatigue, malaise, and unintentional 8-lb weight loss.Vital SignsTemp 37.6°CLabsESR 78 mm/h (markedly elevated)CRP 5.6 mg/dL (elevated)CK NormalRF NegativeAnti-CCP NegativeANA NegativeCBC Mild normocytic anemia (Hgb 11.2) What is the diagnosis, what condition must be excluded, and what is the diagnostic treatment test?

choices.text: Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) — bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain/stiffness in
Fibromyalgia — widespread pain with normal labs
Polymyositis — proximal muscle weakness with elevated CK

explanation:
Correct Answer: Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR): inflammatory condition causing bilateral proximal (shoulder/hip girdle) pain and stiffness in patients >50 with markedly elevated inflammatory markers. Key features: (1) Age >50 (almost never 45 minutes, (4) ESR/CRP markedly elevated (ESR often >40-50), (5) Normal CK (distinguishes from polymyositis). PMR-GCA relationship: 15-30% of PMR patients develop GCA; conversely, 40-60% of GCA patients have PMR symptoms. ALWAYS screen for GCA symptoms. Diagnostic treatment test: LOW-dose prednisone 15-20 mg/day → dramatic improvement within 24-72 hours is so characteristic that failure to respond should prompt reconsidering the diagnosis. Taper slowly (12-24 months; 50% relapse if tapered too quickly).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 72-year-old woman + bilateral shoulder and hip pain + morning stiffness >1 hour = classic PMR
- ESR 78 + CRP 5.6 = markedly elevated inflammatory markers (hallmark)
- Normal CK = NOT myositis (PMR causes pain, not weakness)
- Negative RF/anti-CCP/ANA = NOT RA, NOT SLE
- No joint swelling = NOT inflammatory arthritis
- Difficulty with activities (arms, getting from chair) is from PAIN, not WEAKNESS (strength normal on testing)

Evidence: PMR - StatPearls - Salvarani C, et al. StatPearls. NBK459375. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Rheumatoid arthritis: RA causes symmetric joint SWELLING (synovitis) primarily of small joints (hands, wrists, feet) with positive RF/anti-CCP in 70-80%. This patient has proximal GIRDLE pain without joint swelling and negative RF/anti-CCP. RA targets joints; PMR targets bursae and proximal synovial structures.
- Fibromyalgia: Fibromyalgia causes WIDESPREAD pain with tender points but inflammatory markers are NORMAL (ESR and CRP are not elevated). This patient has markedly elevated ESR (78) and CRP (5.6), indicating a systemic inflammatory process. Fibromyalgia is a central sensitization disorder, not inflammatory.
- Polymyositis: Polymyositis causes proximal muscle WEAKNESS (not just pain) with ELEVATED CK (muscle enzyme leak from damaged myocytes). This patient has normal CK and no objective weakness on strength testing (her limitations are from pain). Polymyositis biopsy shows inflammatory infiltrate in muscle tissue. PMR biopsy is normal.
- Hypothyroidism: Hypothyroidism can cause myalgias, fatigue, and elevated CK, but does NOT typically cause markedly elevated ESR/CRP. Hypothyroid myopathy causes weakness with elevated CK (opposite of PMR which has normal CK). TSH should be checked to rule this out, but the markedly elevated inflammatory markers point to PMR.

Reference: PMR - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459375/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 6-year-old girl presents with 8 weeks of left knee swelling and a limp. The knee stiffness is WORST in the MORNING (lasting >1 hour) and IMPROVES with activity throughout the day. She has no fever, no weight loss, no bruising, and no night pain. Exam shows the left knee is warm, swollen with an effusion, and has painfully limited range of motion. No hepatosplenomegaly.ExamLeft knee Warm, swollen, effusion present, limited ROMOther joints Right ankle mildly swollenEyes Slit lamp not yet performedLabsWBC 8,200 (normal differential, NO blasts)Hgb 12.1Platelets 310,000ESR 38 mm/hCRP 1.8 mg/dLANA Positive (1:320)RF Negative What is the diagnosis, and what EXTRA-ARTICULAR complication must be screened for?

choices.text: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) — defined as arthritis persisting ≥6 WEEKS in a child <16
Septic arthritis — hot swollen joint requiring aspiration
Osteosarcoma — bone tumor in the knee region

explanation:
Correct Answer: PIVOT from ALL: both cause limp and joint pain in children, but JIA has NORMAL CBC (no blasts, no pancytopenia) + SYNOVIAL inflammation (warm, swollen joints) + morning stiffness. Oligoarticular JIA: most common subtype (50%), 1-4 joints, peak 2-4 years, F>M 3:1. UVEITIS SCREENING: ANA-positive oligoarticular JIA has up to 30% risk of CHRONIC ANTERIOR UVEITIS — which is ASYMPTOMATIC (unlike adult uveitis). If undetected: synechiae, cataracts, glaucoma, blindness. AAP/ACR: slit lamp exam every 3 months for high-risk patients (ANA+, age JIA subtypes: oligoarticular (1-4 joints, best prognosis), polyarticular RF- (>4 joints), polyarticular RF+ (resembles adult RA), systemic (Still disease: quotidian fevers, salmon-colored rash, serositis).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 8 weeks of arthritis (>6 weeks = meets JIA duration criterion)
- Age <16 = meets age criterion
- Morning stiffness >1 hour improving with activity = inflammatory arthritis pattern
- Warm, swollen knee with effusion = synovial inflammation
- Normal CBC with normal differential and NO blasts = NOT leukemia
- ANA positive + oligoarticular = HIGH risk for uveitis (requires screening)
- ESR 38, CRP 1.8 = elevated inflammatory markers supporting inflammatory arthritis

Evidence: JIA - StatPearls - Martini A, et al. StatPearls. NBK554605. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Growing pains: Growing pains are BILATERAL, occur at NIGHT, resolve by morning, and do NOT cause joint swelling, warmth, or effusion. This child has 8 weeks of knee swelling with morning stiffness — objective joint inflammation that growing pains cannot explain.
- Septic arthritis: Septic arthritis causes an ACUTE single hot joint with high fever, severe pain with any movement, elevated WBC, and positive Kocher criteria. This child has 8 WEEKS of symptoms (septic arthritis presents within days), no fever, and two joints involved. The chronic insidious course points to inflammatory, not infectious, arthritis.
- Osteosarcoma: Osteosarcoma causes progressive bone pain in the knee region in adolescents (10-20 years), often with a palpable bony mass and X-ray showing periosteal reaction/Codman triangle/sunburst pattern. It does NOT cause joint effusion, morning stiffness, or multi-joint involvement. This child's warm swollen joint with effusion indicates synovial, not bony, pathology.
- Lyme arthritis: Lyme arthritis can mimic JIA with knee effusion in children. However, Lyme arthritis is typically monoarticular (single large joint), occurs weeks-months after tick exposure, and is associated with positive Lyme serology (ELISA + Western blot). Lyme does not cause ANA positivity. This child's oligoarticular involvement, positive ANA, and 8-week history are more consistent with JIA, though Lyme testing should be considered in endemic areas.

Reference: JIA - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554605/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 58-year-old man with obesity and CKD stage 3 presents with sudden-onset severe pain, redness, and swelling of his right first MTP joint that woke him from sleep. He is unable to bear weight. He has a history of hypertension treated with hydrochlorothiazide. This is his third similar episode.Vital SignsTemp 38.1°CHR 92ExamRight_1st_MTP Exquisitely tender, erythematous, swollen; unable to tolerate even sheet touching itLabs Uric acid 9.8 mg/dL, Cr 1.8, eGFR 42Joint aspirateCrystals Needle-shaped, NEGATIVELY birefringent under polarized lightWBC 48,000 (inflammatory) What is the acute treatment given his comorbidities, and when should urate-lowering therapy be started?

choices.text: High-dose NSAIDs are first-line regardless of kidney function
Acute GOUT with CKD — treatment options must account for renal function:
Start allopurinol 300mg immediately but wait on anti-inflammatory
Colchicine 1.2mg then 0.6mg every hour until relief
Urate-lowering therapy must wait 2-4 weeks after flare resolution

explanation:
Correct Answer: Acute gout treatment by comorbidity:

| Comorbidity | Avoid | Preferred |
|-------------|-------|----------|
| CKD | NSAIDs | Steroids, low-dose colchicine |
| Heart failure | NSAIDs | Steroids, colchicine |
| Diabetes | High-dose steroids (glucose) | NSAIDs if tolerated, colchicine |
| Anticoagulation | NSAIDs (bleeding) | Steroids, colchicine |

Urate-lowering therapy timing: 2020 ACR guidelines now recommend starting ULT during the acute flare (with anti-inflammatory cover) rather than waiting. Start low, go slow with allopurinol (100mg, increase by 100mg monthly to goal uric acid Negative birefringence: monosodium urate crystals appear YELLOW when parallel to the polarizer axis (gout). Positive birefringence (CPPD/pseudogout): BLUE when parallel.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 1st MTP involvement = podagra (classic gout location)
- Needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals = MSU (gout)
- CKD stage 3 (eGFR 42) = avoid NSAIDs
- Steroids preferred in CKD — prednisone 30-40mg x 5 days
- HCTZ raises uric acid — contributing factor
- Can start allopurinol during flare with anti-inflammatory cover
- Start allopurinol low (100mg) in CKD

Evidence: Gout - StatPearls - FitzGerald JD, et al. StatPearls. NBK546606. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- High-dose NSAIDs regardless of CKD: NSAIDs are CONTRAINDICATED in CKD. They inhibit prostaglandins that maintain renal blood flow, causing acute kidney injury and worsening chronic kidney disease. This patient with eGFR 42 should NOT receive NSAIDs.
- Allopurinol 300mg immediately: Starting allopurinol at high doses can paradoxically WORSEN an acute flare by rapidly changing serum urate levels. In CKD, start at 100mg (or even 50mg if severe CKD) and titrate slowly. Must also provide anti-inflammatory cover.
- Colchicine q1h until relief: This is the OLD dosing regimen associated with severe toxicity (diarrhea, myelosuppression). Modern dosing: 1.2mg, then 0.6mg in 1 hour, STOP (max 1.8mg in first 24h). In CKD, reduce further. Never use q1h dosing.
- Wait 2-4 weeks for ULT: This was the old recommendation, but 2020 ACR guidelines now state ULT can be started DURING the acute flare with concurrent anti-inflammatory therapy. Starting early improves long-term adherence and outcomes.

Reference: Gout - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK546606/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 42-year-old woman presents with 4 months of bilateral hand pain and stiffness, worse in the morning for 2 hours before loosening up. Multiple joints are affected symmetrically. She has fatigue and reports difficulty opening jars.ExamHands Symmetric swelling and tenderness of MCPs (2-4) and PIPs bilaterally; wrists also involved; DIPs sparedOther Subcutaneous nodules over olecranon bilaterallyLabsRF Positive, 1:320Anti-CCP Positive, 156 units (high)ESR 58 mm/hCRP 3.2 mg/dLX-ray hands Periarticular osteopenia, early erosions at MCP joints What findings are most specific for this diagnosis, and what is the treatment approach?

choices.text: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) — chronic inflammatory polyarthritis with characteristic
Psoriatic arthritis based on joint distribution

explanation:
Correct Answer: RA diagnostic features:
• Symmetric polyarthritis (MCPs, PIPs, wrists; DIPs SPARED)
• Morning stiffness >1 hour (vs Anti-CCP vs RF:
• Anti-CCP: 95% specific (best for diagnosis)
• RF: 70-80% sensitive but less specific (positive in infections, other autoimmune)
• Double-positive (RF+ and anti-CCP+): worse prognosis, more erosive

Treatment pyramid is INVERTED: start DMARDs EARLY (within 'window of opportunity' — first 3-6 months), not after NSAIDs fail.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 4 months of symmetric polyarthritis = inflammatory arthritis
- Morning stiffness 2 hours = inflammatory (OA <30 min)
- MCPs and PIPs involved, DIPs spared = RA pattern (OA involves DIPs)
- RF and anti-CCP both positive = RA (anti-CCP most specific)
- Rheumatoid nodules = pathognomonic
- Early erosions = aggressive disease, needs DMARD now

Evidence: Rheumatoid Arthritis - StatPearls - Aletaha D, et al. StatPearls. NBK441999. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Osteoarthritis: OA affects DIPs and CMC joints (NOT MCPs primarily), has morning stiffness <30 minutes, and is NOT associated with RF, anti-CCP, or subcutaneous nodules. This patient has classic RA: symmetric MCPs/PIPs, prolonged morning stiffness, positive antibodies, nodules.
- Psoriatic arthritis: PsA can affect hands but typically involves DIPs (not spared), is often asymmetric, and is associated with psoriasis, nail pitting, dactylitis ('sausage digits'), and enthesitis. RF and anti-CCP are usually negative in PsA. This patient has symmetric MCP/PIP involvement with positive serologies.
- SLE with arthritis: SLE arthritis is typically non-erosive (Jaccoud's arthropathy — reducible deformities). SLE would have other features: malar rash, photosensitivity, serositis, renal involvement, positive ANA. This patient has erosive disease and rheumatoid nodules — RA, not SLE.
- Gout polyarticular: Polyarticular gout can occur but usually in the context of chronic tophaceous disease after years of attacks. It causes asymmetric arthritis, tophi (different from nodules), and MSU crystals on aspiration. This patient has symmetric disease, positive anti-CCP, and erosive pattern typical of RA.

Reference: RA - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441999/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with 3 weeks of progressive left-sided headache, unlike any she has had before. She has pain in her jaw when chewing that resolves when she stops. She also reports episodes of transient blurred vision in her left eye. She has lost 8 lbs and has malaise. She recently developed pain and stiffness in her shoulders and hips.Vital SignsTemp 37.8°C (low-grade fever)HR 82LabsESR 98 mm/h (markedly elevated, normal CRP 6.8 mg/dL (elevated)Platelets 428,000 (elevated)Hgb 10.8 (mild anemia)ExamLeft_temporal_artery Thickened, tender, nodular, diminished pulseScalp Tender to palpation over left temporal regionVisual Left eye: RAPD present, visual acuity 20/60 (decreased) Why is this an emergency, and what must be done before biopsy results?

choices.text: MRI brain to rule out stroke before treatment
GIANT CELL ARTERITIS (temporal arteritis) — this is an OPHTHALMOLOGIC EMERGENCY because
Temporal artery biopsy first, then treat based on results
Start low-dose aspirin only and monitor
Tension-type headache with elevated inflammatory markers

explanation:
Correct Answer: GCA emergency: VISION LOSS is often irreversible and can affect the other eye within DAYS-WEEKS if untreated.

When to suspect GCA (ACR criteria):
• Age >50 with NEW headache
• Temporal artery tenderness/decreased pulse
• ESR >50
• Abnormal temporal artery biopsy

Most specific symptoms:
• Jaw claudication (LR+ 4.2)
• Diplopia (LR+ 3.4)
• Temporal artery abnormality on exam

Treatment urgency:
• WITHOUT visual symptoms: prednisone 60-80mg/day PO
• WITH visual symptoms: IV methylprednisolone 1g/day x 3 days → then oral
• Biopsy within 2 weeks (still positive on steroids)
• Add low-dose aspirin (reduces ischemic events)

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 72-year-old + new headache = red flag (>50 with new headache = consider GCA)
- Jaw claudication = most specific for GCA
- Scalp tenderness + thickened temporal artery = classic exam
- Visual changes + RAPD = optic nerve ischemia ALREADY occurring
- ESR 98, CRP 6.8 = markedly elevated inflammatory markers
- Shoulder/hip pain = polymyalgia rheumatica overlap (50%)
- Visual symptoms = this is an EMERGENCY — IV steroids NOW
- Biopsy can wait (remains positive for 2 weeks on steroids)

Evidence: Giant Cell Arteritis - StatPearls - Weyand CM, et al. StatPearls. NBK459376. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- MRI before treatment: MRI is not the diagnostic test for GCA (temporal artery biopsy is). More importantly, this patient has visual symptoms indicating ONGOING ischemia — every hour of delay increases risk of permanent blindness. Start IV steroids IMMEDIATELY. MRI may be appropriate for other headache diagnoses but not GCA.
- Biopsy first, then treat: This approach risks PERMANENT BLINDNESS. Temporal artery biopsy takes days to schedule and results take days more. The biopsy remains positive for up to 2 WEEKS after starting steroids, so there is NO reason to delay treatment. Start steroids now, biopsy within 2 weeks.
- Low-dose aspirin only: Low-dose aspirin is ADJUNCTIVE therapy (reduces ischemic events) but is completely insufficient as monotherapy. High-dose steroids are the mainstay of treatment. This patient needs IV methylprednisolone immediately given active visual involvement.
- Tension headache: Tension headaches do NOT cause markedly elevated ESR (98), jaw claudication, temporal artery abnormalities, visual changes, or constitutional symptoms. The combination of new headache in a >50-year-old with these features is classic GCA until proven otherwise.

Reference: Giant Cell Arteritis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459376/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: An 82-year-old woman presents with acute right knee pain and swelling that started yesterday. She has no history of gout. She recently had a knee replacement on the LEFT side 3 months ago.Vital SignsTemp 38.0°CHR 84ExamRight_knee Warm, swollen, erythematous, effusion, painful ROMSynovial fluidWBC 35,000 (inflammatory)Crystals RHOMBOID-shaped, WEAKLY POSITIVELY birefringent (blue when parallel to compensator axis)Gram_stain NegativeCulture Pending What crystal finding distinguishes this from gout?

choices.text: Septic arthritis — start IV antibiotics pending cultures
Pseudogout (CPPD) — rhomboid, positively birefringent crystals (blue when parallel);
Gout — same crystals with different clinical presentation
Reactive arthritis triggered by recent surgery

explanation:
Correct Answer: Crystal comparison table:
| Feature | Gout | Pseudogout (CPPD) |
|---------|------|-------------------|
| Crystal shape | NEEDLE | RHOMBOID |
| Birefringence | Strongly NEGATIVE | Weakly POSITIVE |
| Color parallel | YELLOW | BLUE |
| Most common joint | 1st MTP (podagra) | KNEE |
| Age | 40-60 male | >65, either sex |
| X-ray | Punched-out erosions | CHONDROCALCINOSIS (cartilage calcification) |
| Treatment | Colchicine, NSAIDs, steroids | Same (colchicine, NSAIDs, steroids) |

Mnemonic: 'Positive Blue Pseudogout' — Positively birefringent, Blue parallel. CPPD stands for Calcium Pyrophosphate Dihydrate Deposition disease.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 82-year-old woman = typical pseudogout demographic
- Acute monoarthritis of KNEE = most common CPPD joint
- Rhomboid crystals = CPPD (gout = needle-shaped)
- Positively birefringent / blue parallel = CPPD
- Negatively birefringent / yellow parallel = gout
- Must also exclude septic arthritis (pending cultures)

Evidence: CPPD - StatPearls - Rosenthal AK, et al. StatPearls. NBK557672. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Septic arthritis: Septic arthritis MUST be excluded (this patient has pending cultures). However, the rhomboid, positively birefringent crystals confirm CPPD as the diagnosis. Crystal arthritis and septic arthritis CAN coexist, so cultures are still important.
- Gout — same crystals: Gout and pseudogout have DIFFERENT crystals. Gout: needle-shaped, strongly negatively birefringent (yellow parallel). Pseudogout: rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent (blue parallel). These are easily distinguished on polarized microscopy.
- Reactive arthritis: Reactive arthritis follows infections (urogenital/GI), not surgery. It typically causes asymmetric oligoarthritis and is not associated with crystal deposition. The synovial fluid crystals confirm CPPD.
- OA flare: OA effusions are NON-inflammatory (WBC <2,000) and do NOT contain crystals. This patient has 35,000 WBC (inflammatory) with identifiable crystals — this is crystal arthritis (CPPD), not OA.

Reference: CPPD - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK557672/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 52-year-old man with hypertension and chronic kidney disease presents with acute onset of excruciating right first MTP joint pain and swelling that woke him from sleep. He consumed red wine and shellfish at dinner the night before. The joint is erythematous, warm, and exquisitely tender. Joint aspiration is performed. Synovial Fluid WBC 48,000 cells/μL Crystal morphology Needle-shaped, negatively birefringent Gram stain No organisms What type of crystals are these, and what is the diagnosis? Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.text: No crystals expected — this is septic arthritis

explanation:
Correct Answer: Monosodium urate (MSU) crystals are NEEDLE-shaped and NEGATIVELY birefringent (yellow when parallel to compensator axis on polarized microscopy). Gout classically affects the first MTP joint (podagra). Key differentiator from pseudogout: pseudogout crystals are RHOMBOID-shaped and POSITIVELY birefringent (blue parallel). Mnemonic: Needles are Negative (gout); Positive are Pseudogout.

Evidence: Gout Urate Crystals - StatPearls - Ragab G, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK546606. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Pseudogout: Positively birefringent, rhomboid-shaped CPPD crystals. Commonly affects knee and wrist, not first MTP.
- Septic arthritis: Must always rule out. Synovial WBC >50,000, positive gram stain/culture. Cannot distinguish from gout clinically — aspirate is essential.
- Reactive arthritis: Post-infectious (GI or GU). Oligoarthritis, often large joints. Associated with conjunctivitis, urethritis.
- Rheumatoid arthritis: Symmetric polyarthritis, gradual onset, morning stiffness >1 hour. Not acute monoarthritis.

Reference: Gout Urate Crystals - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK546606/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: An 82-year-old woman with hypothyroidism and hyperparathyroidism presents with acute right knee pain and swelling after a hip replacement surgery 3 days ago. The knee is warm with a moderate effusion. X-ray of the knee shows linear calcification within the meniscal cartilage (chondrocalcinosis). Joint aspiration is performed. Synovial Fluid WBC 22,000 cells/μL Crystal morphology Rhomboid-shaped, weakly positively birefringent Gram stain No organisms What type of crystals are these, and what is the diagnosis? Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate (CPPD) crystals are RHOMBOID-shaped and WEAKLY POSITIVELY birefringent (blue when parallel to compensator). Pseudogout typically affects large joints (knee > wrist > shoulder), often in elderly patients. Chondrocalcinosis on X-ray is a classic but not universal finding. Associated metabolic conditions: hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis, hypothyroidism, hypomagnesemia ("5 H's").

Evidence: Pseudogout Cppd Crystals - StatPearls - Rosenthal AK, Ryan LM. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK557672. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Gout: Negatively birefringent, needle-shaped MSU crystals. First MTP most common. Response to colchicine overlaps.
- Osteoarthritis flare: OA and CPPD frequently coexist but OA has no crystals on aspirate and no chondrocalcinosis.
- Septic arthritis: Cannot distinguish clinically — must aspirate. Positive gram stain/culture confirms.
- Rheumatoid arthritis: Symmetric polyarthritis with RF/anti-CCP. Not crystal-induced.

Reference: Pseudogout Cppd Crystals - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK557672/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 25-year-old man presents 3 weeks after a chlamydial urethritis episode with acute right knee swelling, bilateral conjunctivitis, and painless oral ulcers. He also has a keratoderma blennorrhagicum rash on his soles. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Reactive arthritis (formerly Reiter syndrome): classic triad of arthritis, urethritis, and conjunctivitis occurring 1-4 weeks after GI (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter) or GU (Chlamydia) infection. Associated with HLA-B27. Extra-articular: keratoderma blennorrhagicum (psoriasiform sole lesions), circinate balanitis, oral ulcers.

stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with progressive skin tightening of hands and face, Raynaud phenomenon, dysphagia, and GERD for the past year. Exam shows sclerodactyly, digital pitting scars, and facial skin tightness with microstomia. ANA is positive, anti-centromere antibody is negative, anti-Scl-70 (anti-topoisomerase I) is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Diffuse cutaneous SSc: skin thickening proximal to elbows/knees + trunk, anti-Scl-70 (topoisomerase I), higher risk of ILD and renal crisis. Limited cutaneous SSc (CREST): skin thickening distal to elbows/knees, anti-centromere antibody, higher risk of pulmonary arterial hypertension. Both have Raynaud (often first symptom) and GI dysmotility.

Evidence: Scleroderma - StatPearls - Denton CP, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK537335. Updated 2024.

stem: A 38-year-old woman presents with 8 months of diffuse body pain, fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, and difficulty concentrating. Physical exam and all labs (CBC, CMP, TSH, ESR, CRP, ANA) are normal. She has widespread tenderness to palpation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Fibromyalgia: widespread pain index (WPI) + symptom severity score (SSS). Normal labs, normal inflammatory markers, normal imaging. Key features: widespread pain >3 months, fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive symptoms (fibro fog). 2016 criteria: generalized pain in ≥4/5 regions + symptoms ≥3 months + WPI ≥7 and SSS ≥5, OR WPI 4-6 and SSS ≥9.

Evidence: Fibromyalgia - StatPearls - Bhargava J, Hurley JA. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK540974. Updated 2024.

stem: 32yo woman SLE, new edema, proteinuria 3.5g/day, RBC casts, declining GFR.Vital SignsBP 142/92HR 88RR 16Temp 99FSpO₂ 98% Most appropriate next step?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Renal biopsy essential for lupus nephritis - determines ISN/RPS class (I-VI) which guides treatment intensity.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- ISN/RPS class determines treatment
- Cannot determine class from serology alone
- Activity/chronicity indices predict response
- Class III/IV: aggressive immunosuppression

Evidence: Lupus Nephritis - StatPearls - Parikh SV, et al. NBK499817. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Increase HCQ: Adjunctive; doesn't treat active nephritis.
- Start cyclo empirically: Treatment depends on biopsy class.
- Serology only: Cannot determine histologic class.
- Observe: Active nephritis needs urgent biopsy/treatment.

Reference: Lupus Nephritis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK499817/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: 55yo man, hemoptysis, Cr 3.2 (was 1.0), sinusitis, RBC casts. CXR: bilateral infiltrates.Vital SignsBP 148/88HR 100RR 22Temp 101FSpO₂ 94% Most appropriate urgent workup?

choices.text: ANCA panel, anti-GBM, renal biopsy, bronchoscopy

explanation:
Correct Answer: Hemoptysis + RPGN + sinusitis = ANCA vasculitis (GPA). Urgent: ANCA (PR3/MPO), anti-GBM, renal biopsy, bronchoscopy.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Pulmonary-renal syndrome: GPA vs MPA vs Goodpasture vs SLE
- PR3-ANCA = GPA; MPO-ANCA = MPA
- Renal biopsy: pauci-immune crescentic GN
- Anti-GBM: linear IgG on biopsy

Evidence: ANCA Vasculitis - StatPearls - Seo P, Stone JH. NBK536961. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- CT only: Imaging alone insufficient for diagnosis.
- Sputum culture: Hemoptysis from vasculitis, not infection.
- Echo: Doesn't address pulmonary-renal syndrome.
- ANA/dsDNA: For SLE; this is more consistent with ANCA vasculitis.

Reference: ANCA Vasculitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK536961/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: 50yo woman, 2mo progressive proximal weakness (stairs, raising arms). CK 8,500. No rash.Vital SignsBP 128/78HR 80RR 16Temp 98.6FSpO₂ 99% Most appropriate workup?

choices.text: Myositis antibodies, EMG, MRI thighs, muscle biopsy

explanation:
Correct Answer: Progressive proximal weakness + CK 8500 = inflammatory myopathy. Workup: myositis antibodies (anti-Jo-1, Mi-2, SRP, MDA-5), EMG, MRI, muscle biopsy.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Myositis antibodies guide prognosis/treatment
- EMG: irritable myopathy pattern
- MRI: muscle edema guides biopsy site
- Biopsy: PM=endomysial CD8+ T cells

Evidence: Polymyositis - StatPearls - Malik A, et al. NBK532860. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- ANA/RF: Non-specific; myositis antibodies more diagnostic.
- NCS only: NCS normal in myopathy; EMG is relevant.
- Thyroid/statin: Important to exclude but CK >5000 suggests inflammatory.
- CT chest: Cancer screening important in DM but not primary workup.

Reference: Polymyositis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532860/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: 72yo woman, 4wk bilateral shoulder/hip stiffness/pain (AM >1hr). ESR 68, CRP 4.2. No weakness. New temporal headache.Vital SignsBP 138/82HR 78RR 16Temp 98.6FSpO₂ 98% Most appropriate workup?

choices.text: ESR/CRP (done), CK, temporal artery assessment with possible biopsy

explanation:
Correct Answer: PMR + temporal headache = evaluate for GCA overlap (15-20%). CK (normal in PMR) and temporal artery assessment/biopsy critical.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- PMR + headache = GCA (risk of blindness)
- CK normal in PMR, elevated in myositis
- Don't delay steroids for biopsy
- PMR: prednisone 15-20mg; GCA: 40-60mg

Evidence: PMR - StatPearls - Gonzalez-Gay MA, et al. NBK459273. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- ANA/anti-CCP: RA/lupus; PMR is seronegative.
- HLA-B27: Spondyloarthropathy, not PMR.
- Shoulder MRI: May show bursitis but doesn't address GCA.
- EMG: PMR causes stiffness, not weakness.

Reference: PMR - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459273/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: 38yo woman, 8wk symmetric bilateral MCP/PIP/wrist pain/stiffness, AM >1hr. Boggy synovitis.Vital SignsBP 118/72HR 76RR 14Temp 98.4FSpO₂ 99% Most appropriate initial workup?

choices.text: RF, anti-CCP, ESR/CRP, CBC, CMP, hand/wrist X-rays

explanation:
Correct Answer: Suspected RA: RF, anti-CCP (95% specific), ESR/CRP, CBC, CMP, hand/wrist X-rays. Anti-CCP predicts erosive disease.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Anti-CCP: 95% specificity, can be positive years before sx
- RF: 70-80% sensitive but less specific
- Baseline X-rays for comparison
- 2010 ACR/EULAR criteria

Evidence: RA - StatPearls - Aletaha D, et al. NBK441999. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- X-rays only: Serology essential for diagnosis and prognostication.
- HLA-B27/SI MRI: For spondyloarthropathy, not RA.
- Uric acid: For gout; RA is symmetric polyarthritis.
- ANA/dsDNA: For lupus; RA uses RF/anti-CCP.

stem: Why should high-dose corticosteroids be avoided in diffuse systemic sclerosis?

choices.text: They can precipitate scleroderma renal crisis
choices.feedback: Correct! High-dose corticosteroids are an independent risk factor for precipitating scleroderma renal crisis. If steroids are absolutely necessary, use the lowest effective dose and monitor blood pressure and renal function closely.
Drug interactions are not the primary concern. The risk of precipitating renal crisis is the specific reason.

choices.text: They cause avascular necrosis in SSc patients
choices.feedback: While AVN is a steroid side effect generally, this is not the primary reason to avoid steroids specifically in dcSSc.
Steroids may transiently improve skin thickening. The critical concern is renal, not dermatologic.

explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.
